Looks like I have achieved the pinnacle of burnout - habitual burnout. I get exhausted quite easily and check off all symptoms for onset of stress, chronic stress, burnout, and finally habitual burnout where all the symptoms have become a regular part of my life. It started in university and though I've graduated almost 2 years ago, I haven't recovered from it and I'm so easily tired of everything. Sometimes I get these bursts of energy and I am present and enjoying life, but other times I wish I didn't exist, or like I could run away to the middle of nowhere and live on a farm alone...

Hởi đửa trẻ bị chịu sự tổn thương năm ấy, nến nỗi phải tạo ra 1 thế giới ở cho chính bản thân mình. Nhân vật chính của những trang kí ức đó giờ đây đã lớn rồi, đã đủ mạnh mẽ để phản công rồi dù không mạnh như hình ảnh năm đó, không phải luôn làm chủ bàn cờ như hình ảnh đó, không bất bại như hình ảnh đó nhưng ít ra bây giờ chị đã sống, sống cho chính chị của năm đó, sông cho chính nhưng hình ảnh nằm đó và sống cho tương lai sắp tới. Chị không phải 1 vị thần như nhân vật năm đó nhưng chị đã dựa vào nổi đau đó để giúp đỡ người khác, cỗ vũ người khác, để họ không phải chịu nổi đau như chị của năm xưa. Cảm ơn em vì nằm đó vẫn cố gắn sống cho chị có thể làm được 1 cuộc sống tốt hơn như ngày hôm nay. Có người nói có lẽ cô bé đó đã chết rồi mới có 1 người mạnh mẽ như chị nhưng chị hiểu trong người chị có 1 phần là bản thân yếu đuối của em năm đó, em không biến mất, em chỉ đang xuất hiện dưới dạng nỗi đau khiến chị luốn nhớ và từ đó biết ơn những thứ tốt đẹp khác. Bâyh chị rất hạn phúc, cứ coi như đây là lời thầm cảm ơn cho em đi nhé, cô bé 8 tuổi bị cô lập học cách đứng lên năm ấy. -gửi đến em, bản thân của chị lúc 8 tuổi- kí thư: Mặt nạ cáo bạc- lá thư đến từ tương lai

Talk about on the nose! I am currently in the midst of a burnout crisis at work. A Management change and etc. have left me essentially back at square one with the understanding about how I work and function. After working through covid-19 and the extended subconscious stress accumulating, this has finally started to break me. I have never been good with changes (ADHD, depression, and Asperger's syndrome make it drastically worse, since my mind literally cannot proceed this very well) and working in retail drains my social stamina and ability to even try to interact with people, leaving me exhausted every day. Even if it wasn't a very physically demanding day, social anxiety, stress, and pressure leave me drained. This is the exact topic that I needed to learn more about at this exact moment! Thank you!

Chia sẻ thêm dưới góc nhìn của một học viên Thạc sĩ Tâm lý lâm sàng 1. Rối loạn lưỡng cực được đặc trưng bởi người mắc trầm cảm có tiền sử ít nhất 1 pha hưng cảm hoặc hưng cảm nhẹ. Nói chung đây là 1 rối loạn trong thời gian dài vì sự thay đổi cảm xúc không nhanh như video nói. Thường thì cá nhân sẽ đi khám trong giai đoạn trầm cảm vì hưng cảm hoặc hưng cảm nhẹ có thể là một trải nghiệm rất tuyệt vời Hiện đang có dự án BIGNET nghiên cứu về gen của rối loạn lưỡng cực I. Hưng cảm được điều trị chủ yếu bằng trị liệu dược lý + tâm lý 2. Tâm thần phân liệt: Lưu ý 1 chút là người mắc tâm thần phân liệt có triệu chứng hoang tưởng và ảo giác, nhưng người có triệu chứng hoang tưởng, ảo giác chưa chắc đã mắc tâm thần phân liệt. Trầm cảm, lo âu nghiêm trọng cũng có thể dẫn đến hoang tưởng và ảo giác Đối với điều trị phân liệt thì trong giai đoạn khởi phát bệnh chắc chắn phải điều trị bằng thuốc cho các triệu chứng dương tính (hoang tưởng, ảo giác...). Trị liệu tâm lý sẽ được sử dụng cho giai đoạn tái hòa nhập cộng đồng của bệnh nhân (chia sẻ kiến thức cho người thân, hướng dẫn các kĩ năng giao tiếp, ...) 3. Rối loạn cơn hoảng sợ. Tỷ lệ xuất hiện cơn hoảng sợ cả đời của 1 người là 70%, đây là một hiện tượng không quá bất thường. Rối loạn cơn hoảng sợ được gọi là rối loạn vì người kia lo lắng cơn hoảng sợ sẽ tới hoặc lo lắng mình đang có một vấn đề rất nguy hiểm về sức khỏe. Điều trị bằng trị liệu tâm lý khá hiệu quả đối với rối loạn này 4. Rối loạn ăn uống có nhiều loại, mình nói qua về chán ăn tâm thần đi. Một số bệnh nhân chán ăn tâm thần kiểm soát lượng calo nạp vào cơ thể, thậm chí bất cứ thứ gì đưa vào cơ thể nên không chấp nhận uống thuốc, truyền nước, truyền dịch... Về lâu dài thì có thể dẫn đến mất kinh nguyệt, ảnh hưởng tư thế ngồi, hình ảnh cơ thể méo mó (cho rằng chân phải thật gầy mới là đẹp....) 5. OCD: cái tên nói lên bản chất của rối loạn - rối loạn ám ảnh cưỡng chế. Cá nhân có các suy nghĩ ám ảnh về việc một điều gì xấu có thể xảy ra nếu không thực hiện hành vi cưỡng chế. Vậy nên dù KHÓ CHỊU nhưng người OCD vẫn thực hiện các hành vi cưỡng chế để giảm suy nghĩ ám ảnh. Có quan điểm cho rằng OCD là 1 phổ, tức là mỗi người đều có 1 vài đặc điểm của OCD, chỉ là có đến mức rối loạn hay không thôi (đánh giá bằng mức độ ảnh hưởng chức năng cuộc sống) 6. Rối loạn phổ tự kỷ không được gọi là bệnh do không chữa được, chỉ có thể giáo dục can thiệp. Đã gọi là 1 phổ thì có nhiều mức độ khác nhau, với ASD thể nhẹ thì vẫn có thể sống được bình thường nhưng gặp khó khăn trong giao tiếp, một số trường hợp được gọi là hội chứng Asperger (tự kỷ chức năng cao). Thế nhưng càng về cuối phổ thì trẻ càng khó hòa nhập và càng khó tự chăm lo cho bản thân hơn. Có 1 quan điểm khá hay về tự kỷ và ADHD là neurodiversity - đa dạng hệ thần kinh 7. Rối loạn giấc ngủ: bạn có thể tìm hiểu về vệ sinh giấc ngủ nếu muốn tìm hiểu sâu hơn -sleep hygiene (lo âu nhiều cũng khiến mất ngủ hoặc ngủ quá nhiều - né tránh) Tóm lại, nếu bạn đang lo lắng rằng mình có một vấn đề tâm lý nào đấy làm ảnh hưởng đến chức năng cuộc sống (học tập, làm việc, các mối quan hệ...) thì hãy tìm đến các phòng khám, bệnh viện, phòng khám - trị liệu tâm lý để được đánh giá, thăm khám và tư vấn thêm về lộ trình điều trị

Cũng có một số người trẻ và những anh chị đồng trang lứa tuổi như con và một số người lớn tuổi họ đã tuyệt vọng bất mãn với những thứ khổ đau trong cuộc sống họ đã tự mình tìm đến cái chết để tự kết thúc cuộc sống của mình vì họ không thể tự mình tìm cách để vượt qua những nỗi đau khổ bên trong tâm hồn và thể xác cả quá khứ và hiện tại giữa thời buổi hiện đại công nghệ ngày nay đó thầy ạ a Di Đà Phật thật là đáng thương cho họ hơn đó vì họ không thể tự biết rằng tự mình tìm đến cái chết còn khổ đau hơn gấp trăm lần lúc còn sống đó thầy ạ a Di Đà Phật

Trầm cảm đang được xem là một trong những căn bệnh phổ biến nhất trong xã hội hiện đại. Tuy nhiên, bệnh lý này chưa được quan tâm đúng mức, trên thế giới có tới 80% người mắc trầm cảm không được phát hiện. Theo WHO, ước tính trên thế giới có khoảng 350 triệu người bị ảnh hưởng bởi trầm cảm, hậu quả nghiêm trọng nhất của trầm cảm chính là hành vi tự sát. Thống kế tại Việt Nam cho biết, có khoảng 36.000 - 40.000 người tự sát do trầm cảm hàng năm ở Việt Nam, cao hơn 3-4 lần so với ca tử vong do tai nạn giao thông. Hơn nữa, tỉ lệ mắc bệnh tại Việt Nam ngày càng gia tăng nhanh chóng, đặc biệt trong và sau giai đoạn Covid-19.

Năm nào mình cũng vậy có năm có thầy cô khó hiểu nói "làm gì có ai không có ước mơ" có năm thầy cô lại lo lắng "sao em không có ước mơ?em chưa định hướng được gì cho bản thân sao?" 8: It may take years to get out of a burnout 9: You may end up permanently damaged - your stress tolerance may be permanently reduced for example 10: Burnout is a form of depression, or at least close to depression - so slipping into a serious depression out of a burnout is easy. Em đang gap year (e có 1 vài chứng bệnh tâm lý, có adhd, high sensitivity, overthinking...) Em biết hành trình của mình nên được diễn ra Nhưng em vẫn không ngừng tự ti chạnh lòng khi thấy mn đang làm việc, đang sống hối hã, đang làm điều họ cần làm, mặc dù e biết những điều đó hiện tại chưa phù hợp với em.

Cô đơn nhé nhưng mình hơi mâu thuẫn chút. Mình rất thích việc ở nhà một mình, lúc đấy mình thực sự được nghỉ ngơi và không có nhu cầu trò chuyện giao tiếp với ai hết. Đã về nhà rồi mà ai nhắn cho mình nhiều khi mình còn ko muốn rep lại. Nhưng đến lúc muốn ra ngoài đi chơi thì mình cũng muốn có một vài người thân trong list mà mình thực sự có thể thoải mái khi ở bên họ, ko phải lo lắng mình làm sai - rủ họ đi cùng. Ngày trước có bạn thân, 2 đứa rủ đi đâu cũng có nhau nhưng giờ nó lấy chồng đẻ con rồi mình ko làm thế đk nữa. Mình cũng ráng tìm một n bạn đời có thể sẻ chia vs mình như vậy nhưng rồi mình đã bị quá nhiều stress vì áp lực tứ phía xong cuối cùng họ cũng ko thực sự yêu thương mình. Giờ thì trở về đúng nghĩa cô đơn. Không phải mình ko có bạn để rủ đi nhưng đi thì cũng phải ý tứ, suy nghĩ cho nta xem họ thích gì ko thích gì ... có vui nhưng vs 1 người hướng nội như mình thì rất mệt nên mình chọn đi 1 mình.

em sắp bước vào kỳ thi vào 10 đang rất stress vì điểm số không tăng mà còn giảm áp lực thi 5 môn tỉ lệ chọi cao em cũng đi học kín lịch phun tuần cả ca 2 ca 3 tối về học tới muộn ai có lời khuyên cho em vs ạ

1 Yếu tố sinh học Di truyền: Bệnh rối loạn lo âu có thể di truyền trong gia đình. Nếu trong gia đình có người thân mắc bệnh, nguy cơ bạn mắc bệnh cũng sẽ cao hơn. Cơ chế thần kinh: Sự bất thường trong chức năng của các tế bào thần kinh, đặc biệt là sự mất cân bằng của các chất dẫn truyền thần kinh như serotonin và cortisol, có thể đóng vai trò quan trọng trong việc gây ra chứng rối loạn lo âu. Bệnh lý thể chất: Một số bệnh lý như bệnh tim, các vấn đề về tuyến giáp hoặc các bệnh mãn tính khác có thể gây ra hoặc làm trầm trọng thêm các triệu chứng lo âu. 2 Yếu tố tâm lý và trải nghiệm Chấn thương tâm lý: Những trải nghiệm đau thương như bị lạm dụng, bạo hành hoặc chứng kiến các sự kiện chấn động từ thời thơ ấu có thể làm tăng nguy cơ phát triển rối loạn lo âu sau này. Tích tụ căng thẳng: Các sự kiện gây căng thẳng kéo dài, dù là lớn hay nhỏ, đều có thể kích hoạt rối loạn lo âu. Chẳng hạn như cái chết của người thân, các vấn đề về tài chính, công việc hoặc áp lực học tập. Tính cách: Những người có một số kiểu tính cách nhất định, chẳng hạn như có lòng tự trọng thấp hoặc xu hướng lo âu thái quá, có thể dễ mắc bệnh hơn. 3 Yếu tố môi trường và xã hội Căng thẳng cuộc sống: Áp lực từ công việc, trường học, các mối quan hệ cá nhân hay tình hình xã hội bất ổn (ví dụ như đại dịch) đều có thể góp phần gây ra lo âu. Lạm dụng chất kích thích: Việc sử dụng hoặc cai nghiện rượu, ma túy và một số loại thuốc nhất định có thể gây ra hoặc làm trầm trọng thêm các triệu chứng lo âu. Cô lập xã hội: Cảm giác cô đơn hoặc thiếu kết nối xã hội cũng là một yếu tố nguy cơ. Tóm lại, rối loạn lo âu là một tình trạng phức tạp, có thể phát sinh từ sự tương tác của nhiều yếu tố khác nhau. Nếu bạn hay người thân đang phải đối mặt với các triệu chứng lo âu, việc tìm kiếm sự hỗ trợ từ chuyên gia sức khỏe tâm thần là điều rất quan trọng.

Rất may mắn vì trong lúc mệt mỏi nhất lại nghe được podcast của chị, nó giúp em suy nghĩ và định hướng lại con đường mình đi, nhận ra cái gì quan trọng nhất, và đặc biệt là bớt ép mình lúc nào cũng phải làm những cái gọi là "đao to búa lớn" lại. Video của chị thật sự là một sự chữa lành. Em chỉ muốn nói với chị việc chị làm rất có ý nghĩa, và mong chị sẽ tiếp tục là một người cống hiến trong lĩnh vực này trên YouTube nhé. Mẹ em đang bị rlla và có triệu chứng rất giống như chị mô tả. Bà bị áp lực nhiều hơn vì những lời khuyên quá nhiều ở xung quanh, uống đủ loại thuốc và liên tưởng đủ loại bệnh, nhưng sau khi đưa bà đi khám tổng quát và nhận lại tờ chẩn đoán rlla, thì em và mẹ lại đang chật vật tìm một lối đi cho những lo âu của mẹ. Mong chúng ta rồi sẽ thật mạnh khoẻ nhé ạ.

I went through permanent emotional burnout during the 7 years I had to care for a couple seniors on my own: one with Alzheimer's and Dementia (aggressive), and her husband with BPD, Bipolar, and suicidal tendencies (and attempts). Burnout is usually temporary, and requires rest, but the gradual numbing I've gone through from the prolonged emotional stress, with no resolution feels like it'll last forever. I sort of grieve for a version of me I can't get back. The humor, playfulness, confidence, energy, spontaneity, friendships, the lost time (almost my whole 20s)... it all seem like something I won't get back. I think people who have prolonged and continual emotional stress, with no backdoor / escape / no control or boundaries to mitigate or manage the stress, will eventually emotionally burn out, and start to desensitize and become numb.

I'm a college professor. I spent YEARS laboring under the illusion that systemic issues within higher education were my responsibility. I always told myself that if I stopped caring, if resentment overtook my compassion, I needed to gtfo. I struggled along until January of this year, when a fairly mundane but frustrating problem just fucking broke me. I sat on my bedroom floor scream-crying for over an hour like I've never done before. For the first time in my life, I wanted to un-alive myself. I knew I had to get out before I ended up in the hospital. I took emergency leave and have spent three months trying to recover. I feel like I'm just barely breaking the surface, and it's hard to remember that I was burned out and traumatized for years. Therapy has kept me afloat, but I'm still at the back of the struggle bus. However, I'm proud of myself for detaching from the environment that burned me out, and I'm grateful for my support system, who remind me all the time that I did the right thing by leaving and that I deserve a workplace that won't make me a hollow husk of a person.

Work burnout can have devestating consequences for your health, if you ignore it for too long. That happened with me. I worked a government job for 15 years, and ignored the warning signs of severe burnout. The consequences to my health caused me to have to leave my job before I was ready to do so. Don't make the same mistake. Know the warning signs, and don't be afraid to get some help.

I have had so much serious crap thrown at me in my lifetime. I reached a breaking point when I had to take a 4 month sick leave. As of late in the last two years I have lost 20 pounds, hair falling out and now I am having seizures. The anxiety of just going to work and putting up with the extremely toxic environment, I had to quit after almost 13 years. If I kept going I think I was seriously on my way out of this world. My hubby is having his 6th surgery. Been on going for the last 4 years. Very stressful. I have been off for 2 weeks and feel almost 75% better. I started to get back into my weights again, cycling and I added roller skating to my activities. I am finally having some real fun like back in the day. I also play guitar in a band. I have been focusing on more practice. Sometimes you are the only one that can pick you up, shake you off and then kick you in your butt to get going. Working out daily makes me feel 20 feet tall. I am so proud of me for self advocating and getting me out of that horrible rabbit hole I was stuck in. I have also learned to redirect negative thoughts. It takes practice though. If I can do this, so can you! ❤

I see a lot of people not understand his perspective. You can work a job at a Deli and be worried about Inventory or even health dept and want your place to be tip top. It applies. If you were a cashier and your stressed from the work hours you are given and 6 or 7 days would do you in. Then there is no way to "go in on Sunday". BUT what you could do is Meal prep, hit the cleaners, Clean your house, your car, do your laundry, make the day when you leave work as effortless as possible. If that's not enough and you are too stressed out from it then being a casheir is not for you.

I love the piano example as a classical pianist who struggles with this very issue haha. I have even used the idea of a creative battery to try and explain the problem to others. By making piano my actual job, I have to regularly spend some of my battery on doing accompaniment work or gigs that might not really be that rewarding for me, but I need money. But then unfortunately I won’t have as much juice left as I’d like to have to work on my solo projects or plan more exciting (but usually underpaid) concerts that focus on my passions. I’m actually trying to solve this by doing completely different work and saving more of my creative juice as a result. I’m a couple years off being able to register as a psychologist and am looking forward to (if all goes to plan) having another way to do work (that happens to be very interesting to me as well). I also just think I’m probably someone who just doesn’t like to work a lot 😂 Still figuring it all out.

I work in fast food washing dishes and making prep pans. It’s one of my best accomplishments as I get a lot of praise for my work. Problem is I feel like they’re too dependent on me. I’m the only person who will actually pull frozen breakfast product the day before, as it takes a long time to defrost. I’m also often trying to do 3 jobs by myself: washing dishes, making prep pans, and making breakfast burritos (one of our most popular items). I keep saying “I can’t wash dishes and work on prep at the same time. But I can’t work on prep if I don’t have the dishes I need”. I have someone helping me out with dishes most of the week. It’s usually only for an hour but it helps me a lot. Problem is he doesn’t come on Saturdays, which is when I need help the most. I’ve practically begged for help on weekends. I’ve had literal panic attacks at work because of overwork. Thankfully my managers are understanding but it doesn’t fix the problem

I'm post-burnout! I was a working student trying to manage a high stress career while trying to finish my engineering degree. I ended up losing everything because well... I burned out in more ways than one. Sleeping 5h per day for five years and running on caffeine is not smart but I didn't felt I had a choice. I was wrong! We do have a choice! Looking back, I should have stopped my studies for a year and then find a less demanding job and restart my studies then. Instead, I kept bottling up my lingering CPTSD and PTSD and my physical health was not doing great either. I was overweight and my body was screaming at me to stop! I eventually got really ill and got (prescribed painkillers) drug induced hepatitis as a bonus. Please, listen to your body! I'm still trying to learn how to listen to mine right now.

What happens when you’re burnt-out from working too hard that you begin to see your life sorta fall apart, and you lose all joy from family and hobbies. Every little annoying thing that someone does goes from zero to ten in a matter of seconds. Cautionary tale: Burnout caused me to have a psychotic breakdown when I was 29. Got me the help I needed, got my diagnosis, but it didn’t have to be that way. Could have gotten help sooner, asked for it sooner, been educated on the signs and symptoms of my conditions, etc. If you’re feeling this way, take care of yourself. you don’t need to break to grow stronger; you’re not a femur.

I do the bare minimum at my office job and always will. I never got credit all the decades I went above and beyond, and live by 'no good deed goes unpunished ' now. I only go in, work then leave. I'm too logical to be a full time slave. When I was, I was mentally unstable and always dreary. Nowadays, I work 3 days and play for 4. I finally feel balanced.

Absolutely agree. You don't tend to burn out working 100 hours per week if you're being well compensated either financially or in terms of life fulfilment. this video came at the right time for me. im a masters student and i just started collecting data for my thesis, simultaneously applying to phd programs and training competitive judo. im starting to feel so burnt out, and the fall semester is around the corner. thank you for the work you do andrew huberman

My boss had a burnout and came back after 6 months - only to find that nothing has changed and he himself also had not changed at all. He has been back for 2 months now and he seems pretty similar to the way he was in the last few weeks before he went into the burnout....So the 6 months break really changed nothing for him. Honestly I don't know why people expect that things will be different if they just go back to how and where they were. Clearly that's not the right place or the right job for you, you gotta move on, or do something completely different. Nothing is going to change in these workplaces because someone had a burnout or because a few people quit. The system can take it. Unfortunately.

I've got another thing to add to these unconventional ideas about work timings, I have a thing where sometimes on sunday night I do a bit of work before i go to bed, as it warms me up for monday morning. I found monday morning quite stressful otherwise because you're coming out of rest mode and into work mode quite quickly, this way monday starts off with me hitting the ground running and it feels way smoother

This was uploaded 7 years ago but still very relevant to this day. I've been feeling so tired lately despite having lesser workload vs a few months ago, but I couldn't get myself together and be productive like before. There would be days I'd be productive then suddenly relapse, then I'd feel guilty why I was not delivering as before. She made a lot of very good points in this video. Especially on the reasons for burnout not being directly correlated to the amount of workload but more of the leaders and systems around you. Thank you for this! Now I have a better understanding. The very slow recovery is what scared me.

4+ years of chronic insomnia, around 20 times quitting nicotine, 20+ attempts at sleep resrriction therapy, years of a crush at work playing hot and cold, 6 consecutive weeks of 63 hours at work no days off in a public facing role. Thought burnout was a myth, something id never get, just like chronic insomnia. Now saving money aggressively so I can leave my job. Hope this ends sooner rather than later

I foster neonatal kittens and I frequently run into a situation where a critical condition kitten will seen to be hanging on, maybe even improving, when suddenly they enter a period of extreme and rapid decline and pass away, seemingly out of nowhere. I can be working with them for 8 - 10 hours and they'll die in the time it took me to pee. What happened is they essentially were compensating and compensating and compensating...and finally their bodies could no longer compensate and they just hit the floor. The same thing can happen to us and our brains and bodies with burnout

OMG, that question at 6:29 about choosing with the mind! SO me. I have a loud and strong mind - body, less so. I used to see violinists playing at weddings and think "I could do that... I could earn money that way..." Only problem I tended to ignore is that I don't enjoy being at events. It took me literally YEARS to realize my mind was totally disconnected from my real body, needs and limitations, but loved playing with possibilities for the heck of it. Honestly, I should have been writing fiction for that need but again - do you know how much hard labor goes into ACTUALLY writing fiction? Nah. Still on the learning curve of allowing my mind its thoughts to play with - no strings or obligations attached. For real life decisions, have to slow way down so I don't get in trouble. Thank you - wonderful video!

I was just diagnosed with burnout. I love my job but I have the most insane and mean boss I have ever met. I can barely open my eyes because I'm sooo tired of the sleepless nights thinking about my workload. I can totally relate with what this video says about the importance of social support and good personal skils because I would totally trade my salary for one single nice word, apreciation or comment about my work. Now I'm drained and I'm on medical care and I absolutely don't know how a person like me got here! Even worse, I have no idea how I'm ever going to work again 'cause this burnout shit is really scary.

Even when I tried to do things that make me happy, it felt like I was watching it in the 3rd person, like I was outside of my body and not really experiencing it for myself.

That was great, thank you! I'm a GP in the UK and had to stop work after 10 years of chronic stress leading to burnout. Recently tried to return to work but burnout symptoms returned so had to stop again. I realise now that I still hadn't manage to regulated my stress response and was straight back into sympathetic drive overload! I'm hoping that working on understanding my triggers and working to improve my parasympathetic drive I can learn to re-regulate and practice once again especially since I love my work

The hippocampus bit you sneaked in at the end is very important! If the stress hormones get thrown out of wack it influences threat perception (friend or foe; safety or danger) and can chronify stress. Because the hippocampus is effected integration of past events is impaired. Calming down, reflecting and integrating everything that happens is so key. And also changing your perspective as to what you will allow to elicit a stress response in you and as you point out here so well, when (in the circadian cycle) do you allow a stress response to be elicited.

I don’t think people (who haven’t experienced depression) realize how little you physically can do. It isn’t being lazy, it’s that you genuinely CAN’T do things you like to do. I’m so exhausted, I can’t muster strength to even make myself food. And that’s heartbreaking.

I don't usually comment. The moment I watched this video was actually so perfect. It made me realize that an special person in my life is most likely experiencing burnout. I tried not to take how their lack of empathy personally, but I set a boundary and told them that maybe we can pick things up again when they can settle those things and be present. It's quite hard being on the recieving end of someone burnt out too.

Đã từng trượt ĐH, đã từng trải qua quãng thời gian đầy hẫng hụt khi mọi bạn bè đều đỗ và chỉ riêng mình trượt, tôi đương nhiên hiểu cảm giác của em. Những tôi có một sự may mắn, ba và mẹ đều hiểu cho tôi, động viên tôi rất nhiều. Tôi quyết định thi lại. 5 năm trôi qua, tôi vẫn đang trên đường tiếp tục theo đuổi lựa chọn của mình. Qua những lời tâm sự của em, tôi chúc em có thể vững tin bước về phía trước. Mạnh mẽ lên nhé, chàng trai.

I suspected I had burnout. I think it started in 2021. I couldn’t take time off because the pandemic caused me to be unemployed for that year and I couldn’t afford any time off. This definitely became prevalent in my work, brain fog, anxiety and other issues. I ended up being let go. Which was a first in my entire life. Then after starting another job again because I couldn’t afford not to work. Again, I couldn’t do all the amazing things I used to. I couldn’t stay organized, forgetting things, I couldn’t even remember words, so when I spoke I felt so low. I got let again. Now I’m still struggling, because I have no support. My family dismisses me, friends won’t call back. I’m single. Broke. Every day is a struggle and I’m trying to find therapy, I’m on Medicaid, which we know is probably not going to be funded anymore. Every day anxious

Going to go out on a limb and disagree with you on the antiwork reddit forum. I started my work career around the time of the 2008 crash and every job I had a really abusive boss because they knew I had so little options to go against them. And everywhere paid so little I could barely make ends meet. Even today if something were to happen to my husband my entire paycheck would barely cover the cost of rent. I don't particularly like my current boss so I've been looking and the majority of jobs in the area pay less than what I'm making right now. I would say that contributes pretty well to burnout.

I wish, so so wish, that I was listened to as a child. In an, how do I say, inevitable turn of events, I was diagnosed at age 27 (3 years ago now) as autistic. I firmly believe it is AuDHD, though that is tbd. When I was 4 my “parents” took me to the pediatrician because I hadn’t started walking nor had I started talking. The doc did the classic “metal pot clanging” to test if I was deaf (I ended up becoming deaf at age 15) but I turned my head and reacted. I hadn’t reacted to my now-deadname, not once. Had the test/evaluation for Autism and couldn’t continue after like halfway through because of being too overwhelmed. Kinda ironic. Never knew about burnout until age 29, despite having the official diagnosis. As a recent 30yo, I’m unlearning masking and re-learning how to live authentically, especially with stimming, emotions, and burnout. I truly appreciate your insights and spotlights into and on our community. Thank you, Jay

The amount fulfillment and satisfaction you get from the work (no matter what I kind of job it is) can determine how much time and work you’re willing to put into it. If someone enjoys doing the task required for a job and is compensated fairly for example it’s more likely that someone will want to excel at that job and put in more hours. The opposite often demotivates people and builds resentment towards what or who is making them feel dissatisfied or unfulfilled.

Tựa như mang trong mình nỗi bâng khuâng man mác 1 điều gì đó mà bản thân không thể nhận ra. Bất giác nhìn ra phía chân trời ở bên kia thành phố, sắc hồng ban mai đã lên rồi sao? Thật ấm áp, một ngày mới sẽ bắt đầu và bản thân sẽ tiếp tục lao vào guồng quay của cuộc sống. Tự mỉm cười, cười vào chính bản thân mình. Tình yêu là duyên mệnh, hãy đón nhận chứ đừng cưỡng cầu. Cảm thấy bản thân rất thiệt thòi sao? Rất bất mãn khi phải ăn cơm chos sao? Hãy nên tận hưởng tháng ngày được tự do với những điều mình làm, bởi tình yêu không phải sự ràng buộc mà nó đi kèm trách nhiệm. Tnhien nhớ lại năm đó, lần đầu tiên học xa nhà. Lần đầu tiên bản thân bước ra khỏi vòng an toàn của gia đình. Lần đầu tiên biết được cái cảm giác ở ktx và quen được nhiều bb từ nhiều nơi. Cũng từ lúc đó bản thân đã trải qua 1 chuyện mà tới bh nhắc lại cũng cảm thấy bản thân thật mạnh mẽ. Năm đó mình và cậu nghỉ chơi với nhau “ok tớ chả sao cả”, nhma mỗi khi ai đó hỏi:”dạo này ít đi với nhau v” là như thể giọt nước mắt bên trong đôi mắt đó lại như chảy tuôn ra. Như thể nó đã ở đó rất lâu và đợi tới lúc có cơ hội nó sẽ tuôn ra để giải thoát cho bản thân. Nhớ lại những lần chuẩn bị đi ngủ là trong người tớ lại xuất hiện biết bao mâu thuẫn. Rất buồn ngủ nhma ko ngủ được, buồn ngủ nhma ko muốn ngủ lại nằm snghi tại sao bản thân lại rơi vào trong hoàn cảnh này. Tớ vẫn thấy cậu ấy vui đùa với bạn khác hay là tớ snghi nhiều quá. Nhớ những lần cố ngủ nhma đầu tớ nặng quá, những giọt nước mắt xô đẩy nhau nhma tớ khóc trong im lặng. ko một ai biết tớ đang khóc cả, lúc mng đang chìm trong giấc ngủ thì đó cũng là lúc tớ cởi bỏ mặt nạ ra để giải toả cảm xúc. Mỗi lần ko ngủ được là mỗi lần khóc xen vào đó là mỗi lần tớ lại hành hạ bản thân. Tớ giựt tóc, tớ đánh vô đầu, tớ cắn tay,… mỗi lần làm nvay tớ cảm thấy nhẹ nhõm hơn rất nhiều. Nhưng việc khóc phát tiếng sẽ làm tớ ổn hơn rất nhiều. Tối nào, trưa nào tớ cũng v cả có những lúc đang học tnhien nước mắt tớ rơi xuống, bèn lau vội nước mắt đi và giả vờ là bụi bay vào mắt. Khoảng thời gian đó như một cơn ác mộng đối với tớ v, mỗi khi nhắc lại tim tớ như chậm một nhịp. Tớ có nghĩ tới cái ch*t nhma tớ vẫn muốn sống. Chỉ vì chuyện bé đó mà tớ xé to ra tớ thấy ko đáng. Tớ chưa bh muốn ch*t, tớ muốn sống lắm, may mắn thay bh tớ vẫn ở đây. Hnay sắp ra trường rồi bỗng nhớ lại về chuyện đó tớ cảm thấy bản thân thật trẻ con sao lại có thể snghi một cách nông cạn như v nhỉ. Cũng cảm thấy bản thân thật mạnh mẽ chưa bh kể cho ai nghe và đây là lần đầu tiên tớ làm v. Cảm ơn vì đã ở đây từ chuyện đó tớ cũng đã mạnh mẽ hơn rất nhiều. Cảm ơn vì đã từng làm bạn với tớ! 25/06/2023.

I could relate to most of this during seventh grade, especially during the second semester. There started to be more and more homework, and so I was working for at least half the day. In order to manage my time better, I started going in for lunch to work and it took a while just to get a few math problems done. I thought that lunch was just an inconvenience, so I started skipping lunch in order to get work done... which was barely touched by the end. I started thinking that my hobby was also an inconvenience to work, so I stopped Truck Spotting for 3-4 weeks. I was always finding myself dreading spending another afternoon cooped up inside instead of going Truck Spotting on state Route 3. I was beginning to become easily distracted and my energy always felt sapped. I thought Breaks were inconvenient too, so I stopped taking breaks as well. Since work was taking longer for me to do, I was also staying up past 10:30 in order to get it done. One night, in order to make a last ditch effort to finish a project, I stayed up until about 1:00 AM. One of my friends said that he could stay up till 2 doing work and be fine, so I started wondering if I was working long enough. It felt like a cycle that was only going to get worse, and then spring break came along and I was free for about a week. Then, the same thing happened in the 4th quarter, which was what I was hoping wouldn’t. I hope that 8th grade is less stressful than 7th... I know that you adults out there have more to worry about than excessive schoolwork, and I want to be strong enough to be able to handle your situations without taking rest breaks and burning out.

The three signs she describes here are so accurate. Especially the emotional exhaustion piece where you feel like you have nothing left to give. What helped me understand burnout differently was learning that your nervous system has a capacity and when you exceed it for too long it stops being able to regulate on its own. That is why rest alone does not fix burnout. You need something that actively signals your body to come back down. I work with breathwork and cold water immersion and the combination works because the cold gives your nervous system a hard reset while controlled breathing teaches it how to self regulate again. People who come in burnt out usually feel a shift after the very first session because their body finally gets a signal it has been missing.

I would recommend talking to a therapist as well if having the above symptoms. Often times, people in this state will not admit to themselves they are struggling due to external and internal pressures, but will argue they need to be tough and a decision to do something about it, is taken late when they are forced to do it because of impaired health and possible severe long term consequences. I know a thing or two from my own experience.

My burnout (almost spilled over into depression) started with me 3.5 years ago. I stopped sleeping, fatigued, crying spells, groundhog feeling, became reclusive, angry at work about the simplest tasks, gained weight, no exercise, didn’t see my family, etc. I decided to quit in this horrible market but now have improved significantly. I feel so much better; sleep well, eating better, exercising regularly, working on an independent career, and decided to retire from corporate world early.

i may have missed any statement on this, but i have noticed that in those suffering from burn out, they will tend to feel that even though they have accomplished a task(what ever that job task may be) they feel that it wont have worth in the "big picture". So, as an example, a student who pushes hard to get through Grad School, but begins to feel the affects of the long list of different classes they have to have high grades in, in order to achieve their diploma, may feel after a certain point in the progress that its not worth the hassle. Another example is workers. I know from talking to multiple people, both in my line of work and friends/mutual acquaintances, that if they are put to a repeated task, and though complete it every day, have it return to be done the next day(and so-forth) they tend to lose the motivation to do the task. almost in a form of Sisyphus. Repeating the same task, to accomplish it, and to come in and do it AGAIN and AGAIN. It could have some correlation to endorphins. The initial feeling from finishing the task and having the "i did it!" rush, wears over time. Soon the same task that gave you the Rush of "i did it!" becomes a "meh" due to it being just the same tasks, nothing new to it. Just a thought...

I’ve been a high performer for just over 30 years, but this time around is different. The bar is higher than ever, I’m working just as hard as I did in my early 30s, and I recently hit a wall. Anger, cynicism, mistrust of people I normally like, poor sleep quality. And even after nearly two weeks off, the first day back set me off. It’s now been two weeks, and I just can’t muster any enthusiasm for the daily grind or attachment to the outcome of this project. And the more senior management makes manipulative pronouncements (read “lies”) the more ambivalent I become. I’m pursuing a new job, and I hope I get it and that the change of scenery helps - I hope I don’t carry my burnout with me. And if I don’t get the job, I may just…quit and live off of savings until I can find a break-even level of income with less responsibility.

Severe mental health challenges, chronic illness, poverty. I have dealt with all of these things, and nothing got me out of burnout MORE than directly addressing it. If I don’t advocate for myself, no one will. If it means living in poverty, between jobs because I refuse to settle for toxic work, visiting food pantries, and accepting govt assistance in the interim, the proof is my mental well being, and that has made all the difference. Work smarter, not harder. No matter WHAT socioeconomic tier you are in.

For mental burnout, take a holiday too to break the cycle! Been there done that, I can tell you, 2 or 3 weeks off (or even just 1) can help you take perspective on your work/life balance and help you implement stuff such as hobbies, finding small joys etc. Also, sleep ! And no doomscrolling. Go on a holiday and when you come back, try going to bed an hour earlier every day bur with no phone, at most a book or a partner to talk to. Ask that partner for help ! You got this !! :)

I have been experiencing this for about a year now. I've gotten to the point where I just don't care about anything anymore. I'm angry ALL the time, my fuse is incredibly short, I'm fighting all the time with pretty much everyone (and I almost don't care) I cry a lot, scream/swear and cut myself, I can't find ANY interest in anything I used to love... I’m still a student but I relate to this a lot. I gained a lot of stress because of school and because I had high expectations of myself. I had really good grades and was one of the top in my class, but i was unsatisfied. I felt like it wasn’t enough, that I didn’t work hard enough, and sometimes ill cry whenever I disappointed myself really badly. I didn’t want my parents to know though because I tried telling them before but they didn’t understand me very well and it only made me feel worse. Drawing was something I really enjoyed but now it feels like a competition because I wanted to be better at it. I used to have a really big appetite but now I only eat a little bit of dinner. I felt so much more exhausted than I used to, mentally and physically. I feel really empty and numb most of the time now and I have to force myself to work harder to fulfill my expectations. Everything I did feels so pointless now but I can’t stop myself from working so hard.

After the day's shift done, I felt so used up and had no energy for anything else.

Fighting for something you believe in isn't easy. If you hit a sore spot, people are going to swipe at you, gripe at you, try to undermine you, infuriate you, try to shut you up and put you back in your box. I was starting to learn that was a sign you were asking the right questions, picking the right scabs. And though it's easy to lose yourself along the way, and start focusing on all the people who don't want things to change--for whatever broken, messed-up reasons of their own--you can easily find your way back. By listening to the people giving you a hand up. To the people who have your back. To the people who don't think you're a raving lunatic. Let them be your mirror--not the haters. Let them give you the strength to get the job done. Holly Bourne.

Graduating from all the things long overdue is probably the most tormenting and painful way of letting go every pent-up hurt you've been burying for years. Hindi madaling umalis sa pag-ibig na matagal mong nakasanayan. That's why familiarity is the most unforgiving and cruelest enemy you will face and fight. Those familiar things about them will make you question, "bakit ko pinatagal? bakit ko pa pinapatagal? bakit ko pa lalong tinutulak ang sarili kong patagalin pa 'to?". Minsan, ang sagot ng karamihan, "e kasi nakasanayan". Pero hindi ba't kapag sobrang ka nang nasanay, may mali na rin. Hanggang kailan mo ba kayang tiisin yung sakit? Hanggang sa maubos ka? Hanggang sa paulit-ulit ka nang hampasin at gisingin ng mga kaibigan mo para mahimasmasan at magising na sa katotohanan. Pampahulas kumbaga. The familiarity of being in love with that person will definitely make, and at some point maybe break, your youth. But since we believe in the saying that experience is the best teacher, todo sugal tayo. Kaya tayo nahihirapan, kasi choice nating magstay, and to you who thinks that maybe it's wrong to choose this path, that your choice to stay is wrong, isang mahigpit na yakap para sa'yo, para sa'tin. Hindi madaling umalis sa pag-ibig na nakasanayan. Sa pag-ibig na akala mo'y may patutunguhan. Sa pag-ibig na akala mo'y may wakas. At lalo't higit sa pag-ibig na akala mo'y pwede. Pero one thing's for sure, kagaya ni Carson, makakagraduate din tayo. Makakaalis din tayo sa akala nating panghabang-buhay nating makakasanayan. At kapag dumating na ang oras na 'yon, ipagtatagay kita, happy graduation beh, deserve mong sumaya.

Man, you helped me to understand what was my problem in my life with c-PTSD and explained it so well in all your series, so in the end i finally went to my therapist after 2 years - thanks to you! (I commented it before) And now you deliver me the key I couldn't grasp all the time, that I was massively burned out and stubborn enough to keep pushing me on wondering why my brain always shut off... with kind of freeze, brain fog and even already tourette like effects... Somehow this hint helped me now to know what to do. "The child has now a name" we say in Germany. I don't know why - but maybe I can somehow allow me now to be more gentle to myself and pause the self-pushing for a while. Thank you for not stopping making your Videos! And even if we a crashed somehow - i think of the Japanese "Kintsugi" - where the broken things get even more beautiful when repaired. Or it is comparable to move to another apartment - it is a good chance to sort things out... - AND make them more beautiful. Hopefully. I would lie if I would say I am already there yet - the inner whale is still stranded, but managed to crawl back a bit back in the water waiting now for the tide to come - I am on my way! Big hug from my heart!

Insurance companies single handedly ruin the health care career for me. I just want to help people. I don't want to spend hours on the phone checking the status of a prior authorization and combining 3 different types of codes to ultimately be told treatment isn't approved to then have to somehow try to explain to a justifiably upset patient that their $2,000/mo insurance premium won't cover a $500 treatment for their debilitating medical condition. Some days it really does feel like so much of what I'm doing is pointless. But the days when we successfully get treatment and change lives prevent me from completely burning out.

I’ve been building a business nonstop for a over year, and the only break I’ve had is leaving overnight to fly out of state when my 19 year old daughter had emergency brain surgery. I stayed & helped her recover for 2 months and tried to come home and “get back at it”..but I started to get sick, a lot..and for long periods of time. In addition to this, I made a thousand mile move, became an instant empty nester in the process, and ended a stressful relationship during this time period. My stomach always feels nauseous and I can barely make myself get out of bed to go work on finishing the air bnb (I’m physically building the rental cabin as well) My passion for my dreams seem to have disappeared from my emotional/soul memory and I can’t find the drive to complete my goals. It is nothing less than devastating. Please take good care of yourselves, pace yourselves and try to recognize that being a high achiever requires an equally high level of self care that cannot be sacrificed without dire, long term consequences💞

People say what doesn’t kill you makes you stronger but I’m telling you what doesn’t kill you might leave you permanently damaged. I know many people have no choice but to claw their way out of poverty but by all means if you can avoid being overworked then do so it’s not worth it

My favorite combo to stave off burnout, but you need some privilege to be able to take about 3 days off I'm a row: Day 1, do almost nothing, chill out, don't look at social media or the news much either. Just sleep in and be with your thoughts, maybe light chores. Day 2: full body deep tissue massage, then go to a sensory deprivation salt float tank, then go to an infrared sauna pod. Holy Trinity, especially as the weather is starting to cool down in the Fall. 😅 Day 3: do whatever you want but don't over do it. Probably a good day to reconnect with loved ones so you don't feel too lonely. Do some light socializing in person if possible.

Burnout was what lead me to ADHD treatment... except it took almost 4 years because no one once mentioned ADHD as a possibility to me. I was "depressed and anxious". Channels like yours made me realise what the problem was and after starting treatment less than 2 months ago I'm finally returning to work successfully. They made me realise that I wasn't lazy or incapable when I shut down after taking on too much. I'm almost 34. It's hard not to lament the potential that I've already lost but instead, I'm trying to look to the future. To get my finances and physical health back on track (hi ADHD) to repair relationships I let fall to the wayside (hi ADHD) to be less scared of not fitting in with my peers (hi ADHD) to stop making big plans that I can't follow through with and focus on the manageable and desirable (hi ADHD). So thank you Jess.

Nên mình phải lục lại quá khứ hỏi mình xem và nói chuyện với tổn thương đó .Rồi buông dần dần - Đay khổ không phải cái gì kinh khủng hết. Nó cũng như cái vui thôi. Hãy đối diện nỗi đau đó và chiêm nghiệm nó, nói chuyện với mình mày vẫn bình thường, tao yên tâm, đau vui hay buồn tao vẫn ở bên mày. Đấy là 1 trải nghiệm vẫn luôn luôn phẳng lòng -Mình phải thẳng thắn đối mặt vì , người hiểu mình nhất là bản thân mình. Nếu buồn đau bạn chạy đi đổ lỗi cho cuộc đời,cho gđ, ng thân và bạn.không phải lỗi của mình. Rồi chạy ra đường nhậu gặp bạn,gặp người này người kia thú vui,hau kể chuyện cho họ. Để họ thương mình đồng cảm cho mình, mình nghĩ mình tội nghiệp thiếu tình thương. nooooo. Làm vậy không giải quyết được gì -Chỉ có nói chuyện với bản thân mình thôi. Cuộc đời là của mình mạng là của mình.Khi mình nhận thức được kiến thức rồi thì sống và chịu trách nhiệm

Seems to me like a "job" is rather arbitrary in this context. The brain doesn't differentiate between jobs and just plain lifestyle. People on the autism spectrum often experience "burnout(whatever you wanna call it is semantics in my opinion)" when we try to fit in in society. Many if us have sensory overload issues and just being social is not exactly effortless as it is with most. But here is the thing. Its the same symptoms as whats described here. Which leads me to believe its really about over committing yourself, and pushing beyond your limits.

ive recently suffered from burnout what really helped me is i deleted my social media and dont watch shorts/reels no more. Ive feelt more relaxed dont have the constant pressure of messaging back people or reacting to unnecessary things they send me. While in the first few weeks it feels like you are bored thats great! you have to be bored in order to start doing something that will truly feel rewarding. Its not something that is cured in a week or 2 but with time you will learn that being bored is really not all that bad and can be relaxing. To anybody suffering right now it will pass but be shure to work on it dont put on a "band aid" and continue but take it as a wake up call that something needs changing 😊

I disagree. Actually in most cases a better job where people are valued and efforts are rewarded would definitely elevate work related trauma and burn out. I worked for Walmart software side of things. I was worked like a horse, promised the sun and the moon on projects but in the end passed on like a banana peel after that project was finished. My bosses took all the credit and rarely would give credit. I never got a pay raise for 3.5 years. I never went on vacation. And I never got a pay raise. Despite all promised to me before every major project. In the end I burnt out and couldnt even look at programming for while. If my work did not bash my expectations over and over again, gave me some credit and valued me especially in reward ie salary I would not have burnt out. I still feel trauma and wish I left sooner when I saw other people leave. I just thought they could not hack the work until it hit me.

This only really applies if you have a series of tasks to check off. Burnout comes from the endless treading of water, of it not mattering how much you get done, management will always find more for you to do. Burnout comes from finishing a task only to be told that actually that task is no longer needed and you now have to start over on something that is just dissimilar enough that nothing you just did is recyclable. Burnout comes from weak leadership, uncaring management, and from people who have achieved a position that allows a healthy work life balance lecturing down on you as if they’ve discovered this wonderful silver bullet that is only applicable to people in their similar situation

Here in Sweden we have special clinics for burnout, and the criteria is different than these three you describe here. It has to do with changes in the brain (this has been proven) where you experience memory loss, you can't concentrate at all, information just doesn't stick, you can forget where you live or where you are going sitting in the car driving the same road you do every day etc. It's scary stuff. The body simply "stops functioning" and you can't get out of bed or you pass out due to exhaustion. It's so much more serious than "you don't feel like going to work". Recovery, if you have a serious burnout, can take years. It doesn't need to include depression, but some people have that at the same time. This is a serious syndrome, if you really have burnout syndrome it wont matter how much you WANT to get back to work and life, it wont help with positive thinking or just a little rest. It's a serious, serious syndrome that needs to be taken very seriously. But it's positive that this gets more recognition in other countries as well. Many get diagnosed with depression, but that is a different thing.

This song reminds me of that sad, raining day in Saigon a few years ago when I kissed goodbye to a Vietnamese girl who I met on my trip. The time we spent together was short, but every single minute was unforgettable. After so many years, I still remember the day we separated, her tears, my words to her, and the way she looked when I left. Will I ever see her again? I don’t know. But one thing I can be sure, she’ll always have a place in my heart, and the memories we shared together will be with me for the rest of my life.

Long before my daughter got her formal ASD DX I sensed she would not do well overall in mainstream school like her 3 older sisters. She barely survived 3 days pre-k. So I homeschooled. I was blessed to already have been a stay at home mom for many yrs & custom wrote her curriculum with lots of movement. She was referred for assessment at age 2 bc by age 1 I knew. But she was waitlisted a long time then finally DX w ADHD & anxiety in addition to gifted IQ. She transitioned to online classes free for 10th & 11th grade. She has no live classes & can go at her pace. She has gotten straight A’s. She will do the same this final year. We have no idea how she would or will navigate college if she attends in person classes. We really need to practice with her getting to point B from A somehow. She is quite a hermit. I wish the world was better at accommodating us ND. Thanks for this video.

During burnout the idea of a dream vacation feels nightmarish. Too much to plan, too exhausting to do the travel or do anything once there. Actually the last time I burned out I was about to take a week off work and go and see my sister who was in the country for the first time since covid. Instead, I quit my job, cancelled my flights, and spent most of the week in bed sleeping and recovering. I definitely wasn't depressed, I was past my limits of being able to do EVERYTHING.
Can’t tell if I’m depressed or if I’ve been burned out for years... doesn’t help that school work and socializing both cause me stress.

Tuy khá tiêu cực.. Nhưng có ai nghe xong mà lại vẫn muốn mình chưa được sinh ra không?.. Dù nhận thức rõ tầm quan trọng của mình vs những ng xung quanh, dù không nhiều.. dù biết rõ cái việc mình nghĩ đến điều này là bất hiếu vs người mẹ đã hi sinh gần nữa cuộc đời cho sự hạnh phúc của mình, mình vẫn có 1 điều ước, để ước mình biến mất mà ko 1 ai biết, cứ như mình chưa từng hiện diện, để không ai phải đau buồn, khổ thân khi mình ko còn trên cõi đời này nữa Thật ích kỷ..

Also important to mention.. its not just your job that causes burnout its a combo of everything in your life from past trauma, mental health conditions, an abusive marriage, toxic friends or family which can vary from person to person.. everything in your life affects your mind and its about time we stop forcing people to 'separate work and personal life'.. youre one person with the same heart and brain that goes to work and comes home.. everything in your life has a concious or subconcious influence on you.. no matter how much you suppress, your body remembers hence the burn out..

Big emphasis on finding a hobby. The last time I was majorly burned out, I made a list of the things I needed from my job to feel fulfilled and happy. Then I highlighted what I wasn’t getting, and sought those things through my hobbies. Now, I know that a hobby doesn’t cure burn out — it just helps a person to manage it better, and builds up resilience. Burnout is indicative of a company issue. But diversifying where we get those things on our list can really help!

I think it's also important to note that sometimes what we like as a hobby may not necessarily count as rest and self-care. For example, I may love sewing, knitting, crafting but that may also feel like a project. And if so, it's not self-care. The rest in the self-care must feel restful, more like play and do nothing, to reset. Thank you for your videos. I too work in the mental health profession and relate, and I like and appreciate what you do.

I became useless after I got what I thought was my dream job. I was good at it too, but I just couldn't force myself to work anymore. I'd scroll endlessly on my phone, play games, or do anything to avoid doing work, and I'd scream at myself in my head saying "wtf are you doing??"

I isolate myself because I can't handle people anymore. I can barely handle myself and what's going on in my life. As far as self care, I purposely try very hard to always do it to some degree. I know that if I stop that then I've given up everything. I can't always change the circumstances I'm going through that are causing my burn out. But I CAN always choose to take care of myself every day in at least a small way. Self care helps keep me on track, focused on positivity and feeling good about myself. Which in time will help motivate me to do bigger things. Self care is like a piece of drift wood I hold onto when I'm out in the ocean drowning in life's circumstances. It's not big, it won't get me out of the water. But it's a little something I can hold onto to at least keep my head a bit above water till my circumstances change and I can finally get out of the ocean

Lesson from the video: Almost 98% of people suffer; happiness is the unusual. Yet we overreact to our suffering. Our lives are like an onion, peeling away one layer only to reveal another. We think that once we overcome one problem, it's over. But life is like an onion, and we keep dealing with it. So no pain lasts forever. We're sad, we go out to talk to people, to confide in them—why? To realize that everyone else is suffering, in pain like us. We listen to their advice, and then what? When we return home, we still haven't addressed the root cause of the pain we're carrying. We love 10 people, and we break up with them all. Yes, this person has this reason, that person has that reason; we give 10 reasons to 10 people. So what? Put them on a piece of paper, and there's only one reason: past psychological pain. That pain from the heart is hard to heal, but it's not impossible. Once we know the reason, we find a way to solve it. I'm selfish, wanting to protect my loved ones from falling, not allowing them to stumble. But they need to learn their own lessons from that experience. It requires a lot of practice.

Burnout is more than being tired, it feels like your body forgot how to recover. I remember sitting at my desk, staring at the screen for hours, and getting nothing done. Even weekends didn’t recharge me. What really shifted things was Reset Your Body and Mind by Morgan Lane. It’s a 30-day reset that helped me find balance without making me feel guilty for slowing down. I didn’t realize how much better life could feel until I gave it a try. If you’re feeling stuck in that cycle, just begin with one small step. The change builds faster than you expect.

Careful with that metaphor if you're in autistic burnout. Vacation sounds like stress (the travel, the new place). Instead, think about what if for a certain time, you would have no obligations, all your needs would be taken care of by sb else than yourself and you could just focus on the things you love doing most for a while. See if that relaxes you or makes you cry in longing, then you may be exhausted.

Their discussion about finding the value in what you do really helped me. I'm 32 and just started a job as a dishwasher, and I was fruastrated because it seems like unappreciated, unintellectual work, but then I thought: wait, I'm making sure families and friends can enjoy each others company without the stress and annoyance of cleaning up after their meal. It would be like if I went to 30, 40 homes and cleaned up after dinner for each family so that they don't have to. That is a nice gift to give someone. I'm going to remember this.

I'd like to point out that this dosen't have to apply to work but hobbies and other things you just want to get done as well. I've been unknowingly doing this when it comes to designing my office as of late. I would take a little bit of time every day to look at my setup and go "Hmm if I buy this I can move this and free some space.. etc etc." without really committing to anything. Seeing ideas online in my downtime between work and when pay day came a week from then; I was prepared to get exactly what I needed and adjust my room how I wanted. I think this kind of thinking is applicable to many things in life not just work; because there are a endless amount of things we want to do so tackling them in the most relaxed effort way possible is a boon.

Would you know how long this can last for? I've struggled to find information online (although have not looked extensively) that indicates how long burn out can last for. I believe what you have described in this video is what I have been struggling with this year. I would consider myself to be a relatively high performing person. I took the last 10 years or so to change careers to become a Psychologists. I completed the undergrad course whilst working fulltime, weights training a lot, not sleeping enough, etc. Then did Honours year, followed by a PhD in Clinical Psychology and just completed a 2-year Master of Clinical Psychology, which was even more torturous than the PhD (I also stated the Masters whilst still completing the PhD). This is my first year of no study in a really long time and the shift from working towards becoming a Psychologist to now being a Psychologist has been harder than anticipated. Not because I'm unhappy being a Psychologist - I actually love it and love my work. But now I have so much more time on my hands and I struggle to get myself to do much. I've only recently started to feel more excited to get back into my weights training at the gym and have only last week added cardio back in, but I'm still struggling with overall motivation to get back on top of all areas of my life. I also have ADHD (likely have ASD) and a chronic pain condition, which I probably developed from being an overachiever. I say all this mostly because I'm hoping you can give some information about the length of recovery in relation to the number of years of overdoing it. I'd like to stop thinking that something is "wrong" with me, but that I am just in the slow process of recovery. Thanks!

Suy nghĩ của mình, là 1 người có vài người bạn (khá) thân mắc bệnh trầm cảm, và sau đó mình cũng có giai đoạn bị trầm cảm là: đừng bắt gia đình và người xung quanh phải thông cảm cho căn bệnh trầm cảm của mình và nghĩ rằng họ có trách nhiệm phải quan tâm đến mình, bởi vì bạn không biết được ai mới là người thật sự trầm cảm trong số chúng ta đâu. Bạn nghĩ chỉ có 1 mình bạn bị trầm cảm trong gia đình? Chưa chắc đâu. Suy nghĩ của mình là, sự giúp đỡ và cảm thông từ những người xung quanh là cần thiết nhưng cái quan trọng là tự mình phải mạnh mẽ đưa ra những quyết định tốt nhất cho mình (theo hướng tích cực nha), ví dụ chấm dứt/cách ly hẳn với những sự việc, con người gây ra cảm giác buồn bã cho mình, chấp nhận những việc mà mình không thể thay đổi được, ko thay đổi được việc đó thì hãy thay đổi cách nghĩ của mình về nó. Đừng lãng mạn hóa nỗi buồn của mình, đừng để nó kéo dài. Một khi mình thấy mình buồn hơi bị lâu rồi thì đó là báo động khẩn để mình phải hành động thoát ra khỏi nó. Ý mình là chỉ có mình mới giúp được mình thoát khỏi bệnh trầm cảm chứ chẳng ai giúp được mình đâu, họ chỉ hỗ trợ mình phần nào thôi, mình nên cảm kích những ai đã thông cảm và hỗ trợ mình chứ đừng trông chờ vào họ sẽ giúp mình hết bệnh hay gì, cái đó dễ gây thất vọng, càng làm cơn trầm cảm nặng hơn. Mình muốn chia sẽ 1 suy nghĩ đã giúp mình trong cơn khốn cùng của sự đau khổ. Lúc đó mình đã nghĩ mình là người khổ nhất trên đời, nhưng nghĩ cho rộng ra thì mình biết có những người trong cùng thời điểm này họ đang đau đớn và vật lộn cả về thể xác lẫn tinh thần, trên giường bệnh, đang hấp hối... nếu mình nghĩ mình khổ hơn họ thì mình có muốn đổi chỗ với họ ko? Burnout is easy to miss IMO because it's not just one thing, it's the result of longterm stress over many little things that by themselves aren't enough to complain about. And eventually we get overwhelmed. "Gotta go to work, oh I slept bad last night so the morning routine is harder. Missed the buss by just a minute. The customer has a billion tiny things they wanna change in the project last minute. The customer doesn't speak the native language well so it's hard to even communicate what they want. The boss now needs me to prioritize another task that needs to be finished today. I'm worried of all the other tasks I'm falling behind on now. I mess up one of the tasks so my colleagues have to correct my mistake. Now I'm tired and falling behind on personal projects in my free time as well. Family and friends are badgering me because I didn't call them lately. Tried to squeeze in some gaming but there's so little time for it that I end up staying up too late." Rinse and repeat until you're fed up with it.

Earlier this year, I started medical residency. As soon as I noticed the signs of burnout, I told the team that I would need to anticipate my vacation, even if it was only 1 week. The boss denied my request, and went on to say that in the old days, the work was much harder than it is today, and said that he didn't think first-year residents should even take vacations. After 4 months, burnout turned into depression and I gave up on the program, on my dream. Today, I look back and see that just 1 week away from work, just playing Candy Crush in my room, could have prevented months of anhedonia, suicidal thoughts, feelings of worthlessness... I hope that in the near future there will be a greater awareness of burnout.

Sorry, but no, it’s not just people’s anxiety that’s the problem, it’s also the world we live in. It’s actual bosses getting on your case for even needing an unscheduled bathroom break or as you said, checking mouse movement or not allowing someone a mental break after losing their unborn child because “it’s not that big of a deal”. That’s not healthy. It is not a situation conducive for flexible scheduling and taking things slower. I think it’s inappropriate to suggest accountability is most often on employees and it’s just something they imagine when there’s on a societal level very much work needed on what’s considered acceptable and sustainable work environments for said employees. I think the examples being given might come from people in privileged positions who have the room to control their own schedules and have enough financial comfort to take things slower. That’s not to say it’s not good to strive for these things were you can, but this video felt rather dismissive of very real struggles people face that contribute to burnout.

But I think it might mean that we need to redesign life a little so that it's not so high strung and needing to be escaped from. Plenty of breaks and quiet time rather than margaritas on a beach.

I got burned out almost 18 months ago and I am STILL noticing the effects of this now (on and off). Two of my best friends also had burnout within the last year. It's rampant among people who are the single point of failure at work and don't feel like they can ever stop or take a break because there is no one else who can pick up what you're doing. Hard workers are especially at risk; the GUILT I felt when I finally took time off was unreal. It's so so hard. And when you're in the middle of it you are incapable of asking for help anymore. And employers totally exploit this.

I am self-employed now. Your video (and the resources you linked) alerted me that I might be at risk again, but the nice thing about self-employment is that I have greater power to address it.

I have had burn out for a while. It reaches peaks and I have to go sick or I am likely to start telling people what I really think. When I told my employer this, they refused to believe it. They tightened down the screws, pulled me on taking too many occurences of sickness (4). Then they put me on an "Improvement plan", which is often slang for "Being sacked". That made things explode in me. I went sick. Company cancelled my sick pay. I showed up in the highly aggitated state and said, "I am not fit for work, but I have to work as you cut my sick pay". I had doctors certificates, two of. They ignored them and let me carry on working in protest for a week. Eventually on advice from my doctor I took a week's holidays. I am seeking a new job. Not that they have fired me yet, but no company gets to destroy me and get my services again.

The thing that finally worked for me was getting out of my head and into my body. Cold water does that instantly. You cannot think about your email inbox when you are sitting chest deep in 3 degree water. I facilitate breathwork and cold exposure sessions and the people who come in the most burned out are usually the ones who have the biggest breakthroughs. Your nervous system needs a hard reset sometimes and thinking your way through burnout rarely works.

This is fascinating. I often hear and feel that "2 day weekends are simply not enough rest", which, when they are your ONLY days you give yourself permission to rest, they aren't. However, this 7 day work week kind of unlocks the idea that you CAN rest 7 days a week BECAUSE you are also applying conscientious effort towards work or goals. The 2 days of rest into Monday "catch up" is setting up for failure, but only a couple hours of effort in those same 2 days makes both the days feel better, AND the work week feel less stressful. Truly a win-win.

Andrew, I cannot thank you enough for the content you create. Since I started listening to you many years ago, I knew this was it — this is the place to start. To give my own example: I used to get very sick for a minimum of 2 months per year, and I began implementing things... Morning sun light exposure, caffeine intake timing, Wim Hof breathing, and cold baths in the morning, or at the latest, early afternoon. It didn't take long before my illness was reduced to just a few days per year. CRAZY. Now, with this video, you've hit the nail on the head again. Feeling sluggish because cortisol is elevated at the wrong time of day, plus the right mitigation... That said, the entire section on cortisol, boosting energy, and boosting the immune system completely changed my life. It works for me. Right now, feeling sluggish since morning after yesterday's late and very stressful workout, I've just finished Wim Hof breathing, with a cold shower to follow at noon to properly balance my cortisol. I hope to meet you someday — to catch you after you step off a stage full of people and thank you in person. 🙂

this has been such an interesting perspective to hear. I'm from the UK and 5yrs ago had to leave my job due to lack of support and reasonable adjustments agreed by access to work. I had been with that employer for 9yrs before asking for help. I have numerous physical developmental and mental health challenges that meant I could no longer do my job without support. through the support of my local mental health hospital I sought to get myself heard and although I successfully won my tribunal on the grounds of disability discrimination it still haunts me to this day. I have constant nightmares where I wake up sweating and unable to resettle back to sleep. I can't say the managers name as it causes to much upset. I feel like I'm stuck in that time and realised I too have experienced burnt out. when I saw a therapist I was told I do not have PTSD but I think she is mistaken. I try not to think of it as that but in reality I do fit the prescribed symptoms and I hate it. I hate how I've been affected like so but get that this is how my body has allowed itself to cope with the trauma. I can so relate to your video. Well the bits I understood lol. I'd love to meet someone like you in person so I could talk properly about what I've experienced and continue to experience. Sorry for the long message and thanx ❤

My partner finally couldn't stand me anymore and decided to end the nearly 10 year relationship. I think it's probably best for everyone if I just embrace being alone and not cause any more problems to those around me. At least I got the wake up call to maybe go see about getting tested and a diagnosis. 35, no money, no career, starting over with the help of family. I'm so lost. I'm so lonely. I'm so scared. It breaks my heart how much my lack of ability to take action has hurt her. I wish my love was strong enough, but she couldn't feel it. I must've somehow not been showing it enough; I don't know. I'm blind, I'm dumb. But either way it's my fault. I wish I were a better person. Maybe medication will help, but it's hard to feel hopeful when every single day of my life has always been an impossible struggle just to get out of bed. I apologize to the partners of people like me. Just know it's not our intention. We only want to give and share our love. We're just defective, I suppose. In any way I've learned my lesson the hard way. I never wanted to hurt anyone.

Caregiver burnout almost destroyed me I spent over a year taking care of my older brother who was dying from glioblastoma brain cancer and by the 8th month I crashed so hard from working full time at my job then to go straight home to help my mother take care of him and give her a chance to rest. I had to step away for a few days just to heal enough to come back to help until he passed. Surviving that made me appreciate just how important it is to reach out and say I need help and just how powerful burnout can be.

Though it started as a religious thing for me, the weekly Jewish Sabbath for me has become my antidote for burnout. I'd recommend that anybody apply some of the rules of a Sabbath to their own life. From sunset Friday until sundown Saturday, I'm forbidden from doing any work, chores, commerce, or attending most major events. Though most focus on what I don't get to do on these days, but I focus on what I don't have to do on those days. I sleep in, spend time with those close in my community, read, go for walks, or simply just nap. Work isn't on my mind because I literally am not allowed to touch it. It's probably my favorite day of the week. You don't have to be Jewish to observe a weekly Sabbath. 👍

I experienced severe burnout at work last month. I was expected to achieve so much in such a short time. And since I have a high standard, I put even more pressure onto myself and cut sleep, cut meal and spend so much money from my own pocket to get the best result. But whatever I do, the employer always nitpick and find something to complain about and never acknowledge any of my effort. The working environment is not helping to with the unfriendly co-workers. I was exhausted. It's like whatever I did was never good enough for them. So I became sour and I got fired because I don't smile at work. They said that it's not in their work culture to not be smiley. Well bullshit, no one is friendly at work. People don't smile here. I was the one who made the effort to get to know others while others looked at me and just avert eye contact and not even talk. We are not even allowed to call each other casually. One blessing I got after getting laid off from work is freedom. I finally able to smile genuinely again and feel at peace.

In 2009 I was an IT Director for a private college in the Bay Area. My boss, the CFO was fired for political reasons, and because he hired me, they were trying to make my life miserable so I'd quit. I had been working 12-18 hour days for months because things were so screwed up. I went for an annual physical, and my doctor asked me how work was going. I started crying and couldn't stop. He called in a Psychologist, and I was put on disability. A few months later, I was "laid off" while on disability. I was just so happy to be free that I didn't care about the legality. It wasn't the workload that was the issue. It was the fact that I was working my ass off, and not only wasn't it appreciated by senior leadership, but they were actively trying to undermine me. If you want the best work from your teams, ensure you care for them and that they feel cared for. Burnout is real.

I think that the effectiveness of this approach is heavily dependent on how self-directed you are in your work. I've had jobs where every single moment is rigidly accounted for. Doing boring clerical work on the weekend just meant that I would be expected to do even more work over the week. Thus, increasing the amount of clerical work I'd have to do to begin with. It would cycle like that until I'd just burn out. Contrasting that with now, when I work for myself, I actually do a lot of these things. They work much better in this situation.

I usually don't comment but this really hit home for me right now. I'm 26 years old and burnt out. I'm so ashamed. The society in my country is all work-driven and I've always been an overachiever. I was never really poor but my family couldn't afford going on vacation every year either. Fast forward to my 20s and I find myself with 3 family members in need of care and somehow juggling my fulltime job (which was more than fulltime because of working overtime) and caring for them together with my mother. In January this year I had a breakdown. I couldn't sleep, I couldn't eat anymore. I didn't want to wake up anymore. I also experienced the lack of empathy for a long time that was described in the video and I felt horrible about it. I quit my job and haven't been working since January, I'm just so overwhelmed and therapy isn't helping me immensely. Although my boss' demands were way too many and unrealistic and although I'm aware that my mother, siblings or uncle should have taken on way more responsibility, it's my problem now. It's my responsibility to get better and honestly I feel like a failure.

To my wife I always love you cheers to more years of love and breakthrough!!!
